statement: PKM systems are fundamentally about connections, not storage. The value of a knowledge base grows with the number of meaningful links, not the number of notes. Bidirectional links (backlinks) are crucial: knowing what links TO a note is as important as what it links FROM. UNSAID: most PKM tools store backlinks as a derived index (not first-class data) — this index can become stale. Tools that recompute backlinks on every load are slower but always correct. Extraction signal: a PKM plugin that doesn't update backlinks on file rename/delete creates orphaned references.

Logseq uses Datascript (an in-memory graph database with Datalog query language) to model notes as graphs. This enables powerful queries like 'all blocks tagged #project created this week'. UNSAID: Datascript queries use [:find ?b :where [?b :block/content ?c] [(re-pattern ...) ?c]] syntax — not SQL. The learning curve is steep for developers used to SQL. Extraction signal: Logseq plugins that try to query data by iterating all pages instead of using Datascript queries are inefficient and miss the platform's primary strength.

PKM tools built on Markdown files have a portability contract: notes should be readable outside the tool. Tool-specific syntax (Obsidian's [[wikilinks]], Logseq's block references like ((block-id))) reduces portability. UNSAID: Obsidian's [[wikilinks]] is not standard Markdown — GitHub, VS Code, and most Markdown parsers render it as literal text. Projects exporting to other formats need wikilink-to-href conversion. Extraction signal: heavy use of proprietary syntax is a lock-in indicator.

PKM search is either lexical (full-text search, fast, exact matches) or semantic (embedding-based, finds conceptually similar notes even without matching words). Obsidian's built-in search is lexical. Semantic search plugins use local embedding models (Ollama) or cloud APIs. UNSAID: building a semantic search plugin requires keeping embeddings in sync with file changes — incremental re-embedding on file save is needed, not full reindex on every search. Extraction signal: a PKM plugin doing full reindex on every search query will be unusable on vaults >1000 notes.

Obsidian's Templater plugin (community) and built-in Templates provide note templates. Templater supports dynamic templates with JavaScript: <%* tp.date.now() %>, file creation, cursor positioning. UNSAID: the built-in Templates plugin is simple string substitution; Templater is Turing-complete and can run shell commands — a security concern for vaults shared with untrusted parties. Extraction signal: projects using Templater for complex workflows (daily notes, project setup) reveal the team's structured thinking and workflow discipline.

PKM vaults synced across devices (iCloud, Dropbox, git) face conflict resolution challenges. Markdown files can merge cleanly; binary files (.canvas, databases) cannot. UNSAID: Logseq's .edn database format and Obsidian's .obsidian/workspace.json are state files that frequently conflict in git. The correct git pattern: .gitignore workspace.json and other state files. Extraction signal: vaults committed without a .gitignore for tool-state files will have constant merge conflicts across devices.

The Zettelkasten method (Niklas Luhmann's card system) distinguishes three note types: fleeting notes (quick captures, processed within 24h or discarded), literature notes (summaries of sources in your own words, linked to reference), and permanent notes (atomic ideas in your own words, connected to existing notes). The key insight: permanent notes should be written as if to a stranger who has no context — they must be self-contained. UNSAID: most PKM practitioners conflate literature notes and permanent notes, creating a system that's just annotated bookmarks. The transformation from 'what the source says' to 'what I think about it and how it connects to note X' is where the intellectual work happens. The famous 'slip-box' wasn't the output — Luhmann's 90,000-note collection was the accumulated infrastructure for generating 60+ books. Extraction signal: a Zettelkasten vault where permanent notes still reference 'the author argues' (third-person source language) hasn't completed the atomic idea transformation.

Spaced repetition (SRS) is based on the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve: memories decay exponentially without review, but each successful recall resets the decay rate at a higher level. Anki's SM-2 algorithm schedules cards based on ease factor (how difficult the card is) and interval (days until next review). New cards start at 1-day intervals; easy cards can reach 6-month intervals. UNSAID: the 'minimum information principle' (Piotr Wozniak) is critical for Anki efficiency — complex cards should be broken into atomic facts (one question = one answer). Cards asking 'explain X' train recall of the explanation, not the underlying concept. Cloze deletion (fill-in-the-blank) is more effective than Q&A for factual knowledge. The FSRS algorithm (2022) is empirically superior to SM-2 and is now available in Anki — it uses machine learning to optimize intervals per card. Extraction signal: Anki decks with multi-part answers are sub-optimally structured for retention.

NEVER design a PKM plugin that requires users to maintain a consistent folder structure for correctness. Folders are a mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive classification system — a note about 'Python async patterns for data pipelines' belongs in Programming, Python, Async, and Data-Engineering simultaneously. Forcing a single location creates artificial hierarchy that degrades retrieval. Tags solve the multi-category problem; links solve the contextual connection problem. UNSAID: the real failure mode is when a plugin reads from a hardcoded folder path (e.g., '/Daily Notes/') and breaks silently when users rename it. PKM plugins should discover note locations by content patterns (YAML frontmatter fields, tag presence) or let users configure paths explicitly. Extraction signal: any plugin with a settings field 'Daily Notes Folder' that defaults to a specific path and silently does nothing when the folder doesn't exist has this antipattern.

Progressive summarization (Tiago Forte's method) processes notes through four layers: Layer 1 — save the original source; Layer 2 — bold the most important passages; Layer 3 — highlight the most important bolded passages; Layer 4 — write an executive summary in your own words. The key constraint: only apply higher layers when you actively need to use the note — avoid over-processing at capture time. UNSAID: progressive summarization is a retrieval-optimization technique, not a thinking technique. It preserves the source's structure rather than reorganizing by your own conceptual map. Critics argue it encourages passive highlighting over active synthesis. The PARA method (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives) organizes notes by actionability rather than topic — a note about Python goes under the project that needs it, not a general Python folder. Extraction signal: a PARA implementation where the Archives folder is never used (old projects not archived) loses the system's core benefit of keeping active areas uncluttered.

The tagging vs. folders debate resolves to: tags for attributes (topic, status, type), folders for workflow stage (inbox, active, archive). Tags are many-to-many; folders are one-to-one. Optimal PKM systems use both: folders for PARA-style workflow stage, tags for semantic categories. UNSAID: tag proliferation is the primary failure mode of tag-based systems — after 6 months, most users have hundreds of tags with overlapping semantics (#book, #books, #reading, #to-read). Enforcing a controlled vocabulary (limit tag creation, require tags from a predefined list) requires discipline or tooling. Obsidian's tag wrangler community plugin helps manage tag aliases and renames. The single most impactful habit: a weekly review to consolidate orphaned tags before they proliferate. Extraction signal: PKM vaults with >200 unique tags and no tag hierarchy/grouping are in organizational entropy.

Search and retrieval optimization in PKM requires understanding how notes will be found months or years after creation. Three strategies: (1) deliberate linking at creation time — ask 'what existing notes does this connect to?' before saving; (2) alias-rich frontmatter — add alternative titles, acronyms, and related terms in YAML frontmatter for lexical search; (3) Map of Content (MOC) notes — index notes that aggregate links to all notes on a topic, providing a curated entry point. UNSAID: the biggest retrieval failure is notes that exist but can't be found because they were named by the source title rather than a searchable concept. 'Book notes - Thinking Fast and Slow' is less discoverable than 'System 1 vs System 2 thinking'. Semantic search (embedding-based) compensates for naming failures but adds infrastructure complexity. Extraction signal: PKM vaults where note titles are mostly source names (book/article titles) are optimized for input, not retrieval.

Knowledge graph visualization (Obsidian graph view, Logseq's graph feature) reveals structural patterns in a PKM system: cluster density (notes with many connections are high-value concepts), orphan nodes (unlinked notes that should be connected), and bridge nodes (notes connecting otherwise unconnected clusters). UNSAID: graph visualization is a diagnostic tool, not a daily workflow tool — the interactive graph is too cluttered to navigate in vaults >500 notes. The actionable use is periodic audits: identify orphan notes, find missing connections. Force-directed graph layouts have a known problem: highly connected hub nodes always end up in the center, creating a visual structure that doesn't reflect conceptual importance. Extraction signal: PKM plugins marketing graph visualization as a core navigation feature are misunderstanding how users actually navigate large vaults (they use search and links, not graph).

Literature note workflow for research-quality PKM: (1) read source with annotation tool (Zotero, Readwise, Hypothesis); (2) export annotations to PKM with automatic note creation; (3) process annotations into literature note — summarize each highlight in your own words with the source context; (4) extract atomic permanent notes from the most significant ideas; (5) link permanent notes to existing knowledge. Zotero + Obsidian integration (via ZotLit or Zotero Integration plugin) creates a reference-to-note pipeline. UNSAID: Readwise's automatic import creates a false sense of PKM progress — a vault full of imported highlights that haven't been processed into your own language is a highlight dump, not a knowledge base. The transformation step (your words, your connections) is the one that builds durable knowledge. Extraction signal: PKM vaults with hundreds of Readwise-imported notes and zero permanent notes derived from them are using an expensive highlight-storage system.

Evergreen notes (Andy Matuschak's concept) are PKM's equivalent of software functions: named by a concept (not a source), written as a complete idea, and designed to be linked to and from other notes indefinitely. The key property is that they improve over time — unlike project notes that are abandoned, evergreen notes are updated as your understanding evolves. Naming convention is critical: titles should be complete assertions ('Spaced repetition is effective for procedural knowledge') rather than topics ('Spaced repetition'). UNSAID: the biggest implementation failure is treating all notes as equally valuable and evergreen. Most notes should NOT be evergreen — meeting notes, project scratchpads, and fleeting captures are ephemeral and should be in their own zone. Mixing ephemeral and evergreen notes in the same namespace makes the evergreen notes harder to find and creates maintenance overhead. Extraction signal: PKM systems without a clear distinction between ephemeral and permanent note zones will have their permanent notes diluted by noise.

Daily notes (daily pages in Logseq, daily note template in Obsidian) serve as the PKM inbox: capture everything during the day, then process during an end-of-day or weekly review. The critical workflow is the processing step, not the capture. Common patterns: (1) task capture and migration (Bullet Journal-inspired) — unfinished tasks get migrated forward or abandoned; (2) quick reference linking — [[square brackets]] in daily notes link to permanent notes, making daily notes searchable by concept; (3) weekly review triggers — a weekly note indexes all daily notes and surfaces recurring themes. UNSAID: daily notes without a processing ritual become a graveyard of unsynthesized captures. The failure mode is using daily notes as a complete system rather than as the entry point to a linked note system. Logseq's 'journals' tab (daily notes by default) combined with its outliner format is specifically designed for capture-then-link workflows. Extraction signal: PKM systems with daily notes but no review ritual are capture systems without synthesis.

NEVER build cross-tool PKM sync that relies on file modification timestamps for conflict resolution. Modification timestamps are unreliable across sync systems: Dropbox, iCloud, and git all have different semantics for 'last modified'. A note edited on device A while offline, then synced, may overwrite a newer edit from device B depending on which sync client ran first. UNSAID: the correct conflict resolution for Markdown notes is a three-way merge (base + two edits) — the same algorithm used by git. Tools that silently pick one version ('last write wins') create data loss that users discover only when they notice missing content. Obsidian Sync uses a server-side change log with vector clocks for conflict detection. For git-based sync, each device should commit before pulling, and merge conflicts in Markdown are human-resolvable (unlike JSON or binary formats). Extraction signal: PKM sync plugins that don't implement conflict detection and just overwrite on sync will silently lose user data.

Cross-tool PKM sync (Obsidian ↔ Logseq ↔ Notion ↔ Roam) is architecturally hard because each tool uses different data models: Obsidian uses flat Markdown files with [[wikilinks]]; Logseq uses a block-based outliner with UUID-based block references; Notion uses a database-backed tree; Roam uses EDN data format. True bidirectional sync requires a canonical intermediate format (usually Markdown) and per-tool import/export transformations that lose tool-specific features (block references, database properties). UNSAID: the only workable cross-tool strategy is choosing one tool as the authoritative source and treating others as read-only views or one-way exports. Attempting to sync bidirectionally between tools with incompatible data models leads to data corruption and format drift. Readwise Reader → Obsidian (via Readwise Official plugin) is an example of well-designed one-way sync. Extraction signal: projects building bidirectional sync between Logseq and Obsidian are underestimating the data model incompatibility.
